coredata.dc:description: Phytoremediation is an economical and effective strategy to remove cadmium (Cd) from polluted environments. To improve its efficiency, nanotechnology has been proposed to collaborate with hyperaccumulators in the remediation of Cd-polluted soils. However, the intricate structure-function relationship and the underlying regulatory mechanisms by which nanomaterials regulate Cd migration and conversion within the soil-plant system remained unrevealed. In this study, functional carbon nanodots (FCNs) were modified by doping with nitrogen and (or) sulfur elements. The synthesized nonmetallic atoms-doped FCNs were utilized to investigate their structure-function relationship and the regulatory mechanisms underlying their role in the phytoremediation of Cd-polluted soils by Tagetes patula L. FCNs-based nanomaterials can regulate the migration and bioaccumulation of Cd in the soil-plant system, which exhibits an obvious structural dependency. Specifically, the synergistic application of sulfur doped FCNs and Tagetes patula L. had the highest Cd removal efficiency of 53.2 %, which was 20.1 % higher than Tagetes patula L. alone. The uptake and migration of Cd in the soil-plant system are regulated by FCNs-based nanomaterials through both direct and indirect mechanisms, involving interfacial reactions, plant physiology regulation and environmental influence. This study not only sheds light on the fate of FCNs-based nanomaterials and Cd in the soil-plant system, but also provides innovative nanotools for reinforcing phytoremediation efficiency in contaminated soils.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [Grant Numbers 22306071, 52173168, 22007038], the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province [Grant Numbers ZR2023QB124, ZR2020LFG009], the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation [Grant Number 2023M731303], the Postdoctoral Research Startup Fund of University of Jinan [Grant Number XBH2214], the Major Scientific and Technological Innovation Project of Shandong Province [Grant Number 2021CXGC010603], and the Jinan City University Integration Development Strategy Project [Grant Number JNSX2023021]. The authors extend their gratitude to Shiyanjia lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for providing invaluable assistance with the environmental and analysis. Soil heavy metal contamination is a major global environmental problem. Cd has attracted widest attention in soil science due to its extremely toxicity, un-degradability, and high-mobility in the soil-plant system. As a typical hazardous material, Cd remediation in contaminated soils is urgent. This study employed functional carbon nanodots as a \u201Cnanotool\u201D to promote the phytoremediation of Cd-polluted soils, and revealed the synergistic promotion mechanism and structure-function relationship of nonmetallic atoms doped carbon nanodots driving Tagetes patula L. to remediate Cd-contaminated soils. The results provide new insights into the fate of nanomaterials and Cd in the soil-plant system.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [Grant Numbers 22306071, 52173168, 22007038], the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province [Grant Numbers ZR2023QB124, ZR2020LFG009], the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation [Grant Number 2023M731303], the Postdoctoral Research Startup Fund of University of Jinan [Grant Number XBH2214], the Major Scientific and Technological Innovation Project of Shandong Province [Grant Number 2021CXGC010603], and the Jinan City University Integration Development Strategy Project [Grant Number JNSX2023021]. The authors extend their gratitude to Shiyanjia lab ( www.shiyanjia.com ) for providing invaluable assistance with the environmental and analysis.

(c) Cd contents in plant. (d) Bioconcentration factor (BCF) and transfer factor (TF) of Cd in each treatment. (e) Cd contents in subcellular fraction. Data from three experiments (N = 3) are reported as mean ± SD. Different lowercase and uppercase letters indicate significant differences among treatments at P < 0.05 or P < 0.1.

Data from three experiments (N = 3) are reported as mean ± SD. Different lowercase and uppercase letters indicate significant differences among treatments at P < 0.1.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Phytoremediation is an economical and effective strategy to remove cadmium (Cd) from polluted environments. To improve its efficiency, nanotechnology has been proposed to collaborate with hyperaccumulators in the remediation of Cd-polluted soils. However, the intricate structure-function relationship and the underlying regulatory mechanisms by which nanomaterials regulate Cd migration and conversion within the soil-plant system remained unrevealed. In this study, functional carbon nanodots (FCNs) were modified by doping with nitrogen and (or) sulfur elements. The synthesized nonmetallic atoms-doped FCNs were utilized to investigate their structure-function relationship and the regulatory mechanisms underlying their role in the phytoremediation of Cd-polluted soils by Tagetes patula L. FCNs-based nanomaterials can regulate the migration and bioaccumulation of Cd in the soil-plant system, which exhibits an obvious structural dependency. Specifically, the synergistic application of sulfur doped FCNs and Tagetes patula L. had the highest Cd removal efficiency of 53.2 %, which was 20.1 % higher than Tagetes patula L. alone. The uptake and migration of Cd in the soil-plant system are regulated by FCNs-based nanomaterials through both direct and indirect mechanisms, involving interfacial reactions, plant physiology regulation and environmental influence. This study not only sheds light on the fate of FCNs-based nanomaterials and Cd in the soil-plant system, but also provides innovative nanotools for reinforcing phytoremediation efficiency in contaminated soils.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the rapid development of industries such as mining and automobile manufacturing, large amounts of heavy metals (HMs) enter the environment [1]. Heavy metals pollution in the environment has become an urgent problem that adversely affects plants, animals and human health [2]. Cadmium (Cd) is one of the most damaging toxic heavy metals that has attracted the most attention in soil science due to its extremely toxicity to living organisms, un-degradability, long half-times, and high mobility in the soil-plant system [2-4]. Therefore, the remediation of Cd contaminated soils is crucial to reduce associated risks and maintain ecological health [5]. Phytoremediation, which uses hyperaccumulator to absorb and accumulate Cd from the environment, has been extensively studied and utilized in heavy metals contamination because it is economical, effective, eco-friendly and sustainable [6]. Phytoremediation enriches the available Cd in the environment in stems and leaves, which can not only extract soil Cd for resource utilization, but also remediate large-scale polluted sites through plant community [7,8]. However, the widespread application of phytoremediation is limited by the limitations especially low efficiency and long remediation time [9]. Therefore, it is urgent to develop eco-friendly strategies to elevate the efficiency of phytoremediation [9].

The key factors affecting the remediation efficiency of phytoremediation technology are: the bioavailability of soil Cd, the heavy metals tolerance and biomass of plants, and the phytoextraction ability of plants to Cd [10]. Exogenous substances such as EDTA chelating agent, organic acids and plant hormones were often used to improve the phytoremediation efficiency in previous studies [11,12]. However, these soil additives are applied in large amounts, which can easily damage the soil structure and cause secondary toxicity to organisms [11]. With the advancement of nanotechnology, nanomaterials (NMs) are playing an increasingly important role in promoting plant to tolerant stress and improving soil quality, thus providing a new idea for the enhancement of phytoextraction technology [13]. On one hand, NMs can improve the bioavailability of heavy metals. Compared with conventional materials, NMs possess high specific surface area, modifiable surface structure and nano-size effect (< 100 nm), resulting in controllable and excellent reactivity and absorptivity [14]. These specific properties enable NMs to not only regulate the availability of heavy metals through adsorption/desorption processes in soils, but also cross biological barriers and act as carriers for transporting heavy metals to plants [13]. For example, the morphology and transformation of Cd on biochar surface had obvious size effects, which manifested that the proportion of bioavailable Cd on the nano-biochar surface is higher than that on large biochar particles [15]. On the other hand, NMs can enhance the tolerance and bioaccumulation ability of plants to heavy metals through physiological regulation [12]. For example, 100 mg kg-1 of zero-valent iron nanoparticles (nZVI) and 500 mg kg-1 of carbon nanotubes (CNTs) can effectively promote plant growth under heavy metals stress, and improve the absorption and transport of heavy metals in plant, thereby improving the phytoremediation efficiency to Pb and Cd in the environment [16-18]. Although NMs synergistic phytoremediation is an effective and eco-friendly strategy for heavy metals remediation, it is difficult to achieve synergistic regulation in heavy metals bioavailability, plant tolerance and bioaccumulation ability at the same time. In addition, the conventional NMs are limited in practical application due to their high costs, large dosages, environmental risks and limited universality [19]. For example, despite nZVI improved the phytoremediation efficiency of Lolium perenne L. to Pb in sediments [14], they also reduced the Zn bioavailability and inhibited the phytoextraction of sunflowers in mining areas [20]. Moreover, the biological toxicities of some NMs and their synergistic toxicities with heavy metals cannot be ignored in their application. For instance, nano-polystyrene may cause secondary pollution or even amplify the heavy metals’ toxicity in environment [21]. Therefore, it is crucial to develop convenient, environmentally friendly and universally applicable NMs for enhancing phytoremediation efficiency through synergistic regulation of Cd bioavailability, plant tolerance and bioaccumulation capacity.

The structure and property of NMs affect their environmental behaviors with heavy metals, including the migration/transformation of heavy metals and plant physiological responses [6]. First, the size, morphology and surface structure of NMs determine the types and distribution of the interfacial reaction sites with heavy metals. Then, the interfacial behaviors of NMs with heavy metals, such as adsorption/desorption processes, existence and agglomeration forms, directly affect the bioavailability and migration/transformation processes of heavy metals in the soil-plant system [19,22]. For instance, the adsorption of graphene oxide to Cd2+ and Pb2+ was significantly enhanced by doping oxygen-containing functional groups on their surface [23]. Our previous result also demonstrated that functional carbon nanodots (FCNs) rich in carboxyl and hydroxyl groups improved the bioavailability of Pb and Cd in water [24]. This was because that functional groups especially carboxyl endowed FCNs more interfacial active sites, higher hydrophilicity and electronegativity, which resulted in stronger electrostatic attraction and complexation reactivity to cations. Thus, FCNs could capture Cd2+ and Pb2+ and act as a carrier to deliver heavy metals into plants [24]. Second, the physiochemical properties of NMs including the surface charges and functional groups also influence their bio-functionality [19]. For example, positively charged nanoparticles are more easily transmitted into plant cells due to their stronger affinity for the negatively charged plant cell wall and the higher negative transmembrane potential [25]. Our previous studies also proved that carboxyl groups played important roles in the processes of FCNs regulating plant growth and stress tolerance, which is attributed by the indispensable effects of carboxyl on FCNs’ function as nano-enzyme and bio-enzyme carrier [26-28]. The previous studies also demonstrated that FCNs that synthesized through a green electrolysis method could increase plant growth, alleviate oxidative damage, and maintain enzyme function in plants under stress [29,30]. Thus, FCNs are of great potential in promoting phytoextraction. In order to facilitate the structural modification and efficient utilization of FCNs in coupling phytoremediation, it is crucial to reveal the structure-function relationship of FCNs regulating phytoextraction in the soil-plant system.

As a result, this study aims to reveal the structure-function relationship and regulation mechanisms by which FCNs-based NMs (FNMs) regulating Cd migration, transformation and phytoremediation in the soil-plant system. Tagetes patula L., a species of common landscape plant, was chosen as the potential hyperaccumulator for Cd in this research [31]. The components and surface properties of FNMs were modified by doping non-metallic elements including nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) on FCNs with a hydrothermal method. The structure-function relationship and regulation mechanisms of FNMs on Tagetes patula L. remediating Cd-contaminated soil were explored by integrating their different influences on phytoremediation efficiencies, plant physiological processes, soil properties and microbial environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and methods
Modification and characterization of FNMs
ce:section.ce:para: FCNs used in this study were synthesized using only graphite and water with our previous reported electrolysis method [32]. Modified FCNs (MFCNs) including sulfur doped FCNs (S-FCNs), nitrogen doped FCNs (N-FCNs), and sulfur-nitrogen co-doped FCNs (S,N-FCNs) were synthesized from FCNs as precursor with the green hydrothermal methods. Specifically, sodium sulfite, ammonia and thiourea were used as S source, N source and S & N source to synthesize S-FCNs, N-FCNs and S,N-FCNs, respectively. And their mass ratio to FCNs in the reactive mixture was 1:1. The mixture was added in the hydrothermal reactor and reacted at 120 °C for 10 h. The synthesized FNMs should be stored in fridge at 4 °C for the further pot-experiments. The morphology and structural characteristics of FNMs were analyzed using several techniques including transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100plus, Japan), Raman spectrometer (LabRam HR Evolution, Japan), Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Nicolet iS50, America) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250XI, America). Zeta potentials of materials were measured using a Malvin laser particle size analyzer (Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90, England).
More details about the synthesis and characterization methods are described in supplementary materials (SMs).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental design and growth conditions
ce:section.ce:para: The soils used in this study were collected from a farmland in northern China. To create simulated Cd-polluted soils, the grinded CdCl2 was spiked into the soils. The initial properties of the Cd-polluted soils are: pH of 7.83, Cd content of 12.5 mg kg-1, soil organic matters (SOM) of 48.7 g kg-1, cation exchange capacity (CEC) of 19.6 cmol kg-1, available nitrogen (AN) of 145 mg kg-1, available phosphate (AP) of 86.1 mg kg-1, and available potassium (AK) of 489 mg kg-1.

Uniform-sized Tagetes patula L. seedlings of 4-week-old were transplanted into pots and cultivated in a greenhouse. The condition of greenhouse was: day/night temperature 28 °C/25 °C, 16 h light/8 h dark and 75 % humidity. After transplanting, the MFCNs stored at 4 ℃ were diluted and watered directly to the soils at a content of 30 mg kg-1 soils. The treatments in soils were grouped as CT (without Cd or FNMs), Cd (12.5 mg kg-1 Cd without FNMs), CdF (12.5 mg kg-1 Cd + 30 mg kg-1 FCNs), CdS (12.5 mg kg-1 Cd + 30 mg kg-1 S-FCNs), CdN (12.5 mg kg-1 Cd + 30 mg kg-1 N-FCNs) and CdSN (12.5 mg kg-1 Cd + 30 mg kg-1 S,N-FCNs). Three replicates for each treatment and weekly plant phenotyping records were performed. Each pot was arranged in a completely randomized block design and changed the location every 7 days. After 47 days of cultivation, plant and soil samples were collected at the flowering stage of Tagetes patula L. for the further determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of plant physiological parameters
ce:section.ce:para: After harvest and rinsing with flowed water, plant height was measured using a steel ruler manually. Fresh weight (FW) of plant tissue was weighed after blotting the surface moisture on plant [30]. Contents of Cd and nutrient elements (including Ca, Fe and Zn) in dry leaves and roots were determined by inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP-OES, NexlON 1000 G, America) after digesting with nitric acid and perchloric acid. The bioconcentration factor (BCF), transfer factor (TF) and remediation efficiency were calculated based on the Cd contents in plant and soils. The chlorophyll (Chl) in leaves was extracted by ethanol and measured by spectrophotometry at 652 nm using an ultraviolet and visible spectrophotometer (UV–vis, Thermo Evolution 220, America) [33]. The activities of enzymatic antioxidant system, including peroxidase (POD), catalase (CAT), superoxide dismutase (SOD) and ascorbate peroxidase (APX), were measured by spectrophotometry using reagent kits (Solarbio Science & Technology Co., LTD, China). Malondialdehyde (MDA) content was determined in fresh leaves using thiobarbituric acid [34]. The ascorbic acid (AsA) content was determined with fast blue salt B at 420 nm using UV–vis [29]. The total antioxidant capacity was determined according to the change in absorbance using the DPPH method and recorded at 515 nm [35]. The organic acid assay was performed using a High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC, Shimadzu LC-20AD), based on the method in [36] with some modifications. Subcellular isolation was performed by hypothermal differential centrifugation, and the Cd content in cell wall, cytoplasm and organelle were determined by ICP-OES. Relative expressions of heavy metals ATPase (HMA) genes were analyzed using the RT-qPCR method (primers listed in Table S3) [37]. More details are provided in SMs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of soil physicochemical properties in rhizosphere
ce:section.ce:para: Rhizosphere soil samples were collected after plant harvest. The soil pH and electrical conductivity (EC) (mass ratio of soil to water is 1:5) were measured using a pH meter. Soil cation exchange capacity (CEC) was analyzed by ammonium chloride-ammonium acetate exchange method [24]. Soil organic carbon (SOC) content was determined with potassium dichromate by volumetric method [38]. AN content was determined by the alkali diffusion method, and AP content was detected by molybdenum antimony anti colorimetry [29]. AK content was tested by ICP-OES after digesting soil samples with hydrofluoric acid - nitric acid - perchloric acid (5:3:1) [39]. Activities of soil polyphenol oxidase (S-PPO), soil catalase (S-CAT), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), and urease (UE) were detected by spectrophotometry using commercial kits purchased from Solarbio Science & Technology Co., LTD, China. Soil bacterial community structure and diversity were detected by high-throughput sequencing technology [40].
Details of these determination processes are described in SMs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Statistical Analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Statistical analyses were performed by SPSS 27.0. The collected data were presented as mean values of three independent replicates with standard deviation (SD). Differences between treatments were statistically analyzed via Duncan's test and one-way analysis (ANOVA). According to the least significant difference (LSD) test, the significant differences (P < 0.05, or P＜0.1) were labeled with different lowercase letters in the figures and tables.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results
Characteristics of FNMs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized FCNs and MFCNs were nanodots with multiple functional groups on their surface based on the results in Fig. 1, Fig. S1& S2, Table S1 & S2. The maximum sizes of FCNs, S-FCNs, N-FCNs and S,N-FCNs were about 5.0 nm, 4.5 nm, 7.0 nm and 5.0 nm according to TEM photos (Fig. 1a and S1a-d). From FTIR (Fig. 1b and Table S1) and XPS results (Fig. 1d-g), FCNs were rich in oxygen-containing functional groups, including carboxyl groups, esters, carbonyl groups, hydroxyl groups, ether bonds, etc. S atoms were doped on S-FCN mainly in the form of C-S-C bonds, and the other groups on S-FCNs were hydroxyl, carboxyl, ester, carbonyl and sulfonyl group etc. The main chemical groups on the surface of N-FCNs were CO, C-O, amino N, pyrrole N and pyridine N. The surface of S,N-FCNs contained the most diverse organic groups, such as carboxyl, ester, carbonyl, hydroxyl, ether bonds, amino N, pyridine N, C-S bonds, and sulfone groups. The Raman spectra of materials (Fig. 1c) showed that the intensity ratio of the D peak to G peak increased after doping, reflecting the increased surface defects on graphene structure. Based on the zeta potentials in Fig. 1h, N-FCNs have the lowest zeta potential at −38.2 mV, followed by S-FCN, S,N-FCN and FCN at −28.7, −25.5, and −23.5 mV, respectively. As the stability of NMs is positive correlated with the absolute value of zeta potential, the stability and electronegativity of MFCNs enhanced with modification. The acidity of MFCNs decreased compared to FCNs, which was evidenced by the increased pH values. Specifically, pH of 2.3 mg mL-1 FCNs, S-FCNs, N-FCNs, and S, N-FCNs solution were 2.3, 3.9, 5.9, and 3.3, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of FNMs on the growth and ornamental value of Tagetes patula L. in Cd contaminated soils
ce:section.ce:para: The phenotype and biomass of Tagetes patula L. exposed to 12.5 mg kg-1 Cd are shown in Fig. 2 and S3. The plant height in Cd group increased compared with the CT group, but addition of FNMs mitigated the change with the decreased plant heights (Fig. 2a). Compared with the CT group, the biomass of aboveground parts and roots in Cd group were significantly reduced by 56.1 % and 36.0 %, respectively (Fig. 2b, P < 0.05). In contrast with the Cd group, the biomass of aboveground part in CdF group incremented with 33.1 %, and which in CdS group and CdSN group notedly increased by 80.6 % and 64.0 %, respectively (P < 0.05). However, FNMs showed no significant influences on root biomasses under Cd stress (Fig. 2b, P < 0.05). As shown in Fig. 2c, the number of flowers and flower buds in the Cd group was reduced by 51.2 % than CT group. After applicating with FNMs, the ornamental value of Tagetes patula L. in CdS and CdSN groups enhanced with the flowers and flower buds numbers increased by 58.7 % and 52.4 %, respectively (Fig. 2c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of FNMs on the phytoremediation of Cd-polluted soils by Tagetes patula L.
ce:section.ce:para: From Fig. 3, application of FCNs, S-FCNs and S,N-FCNs significantly improved the remediation efficiency of Tagetes patula L. and reduced the Cd contents in rhizosphere soils. The promotion effect of S-FCNs on phytoremediation of Cd was the most obvious, with the highest Cd removal efficiency and BF in CdS group (Fig. 3). In CdS group, the soil Cd contents decreased by 30.1 % and the Cd removal efficiency elevated by 20.1 % than Cd group. The Cd removal rates of CdF, CdS and CdSN were 49.0 %, 53.2 % and 45.1 %, respectively. Besides, FCNs and S,N-FCNs significantly reduced the soil Cd contents in CdF and CdSN groups by 23.7 % and 18.0 %, and promote the Cd removal efficiency by 15.9 % and 12.0 % than Cd group (P < 0.05). As shown in Fig. 3c, the Cd contents in the roots of Tagetes patula L. were raised by FNMs, with increased rates of 8.23 %, 71.1 %, 46.3 % and 39.8 % in CdF, CdS, CdN and CdSN groups, respectively. The Cd contents in the aerial parts of Tagetes patula L. were increased by 2.44 % and 11.1 % in CdS and CdN groups, but which was decreased in CdF group. It was evident that S-FCNs and N-FCNs significantly enhanced BCFs of Tagetes patula L. to soil Cd, with the boosting effects of 1.50 folds in CdS group and 1.22 folds in CdN group contrasted with Cd group (Fig. 3d). However, the TFs were decreased with FNMs addition. The TFs in CdF, CdS, CdN and CdSN groups were decreased by 18.4 %, 40.1 %, 24.1 % and 30.3 %, respectively. At the subcellular scale (Fig. 3e), although FNMs did not significantly affect the Cd contents in cell wall and cytoplasm, they changed the Cd contents in organelles obviously. S,N-FCNs reduced the Cd content in organelles and cytoplasm by 82.9 % and 7.05 %, respectively. S-FCNs and N-FCNs significantly increased the Cd content in organelles, with incremented rates of 174 % and 141 % (P < 0.1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of FNMs on the acquisition of other divalent metals in Tagetes patula L.
ce:section.ce:para: The contents of divalent nutrient elements including calcium (Ca), iron (Fe), and zinc (Zn) in plants are shown in Table 1. There were significant differences in the content of Ca in leaves and roots with the addition of FNMs (P < 0.05). Specifically, the Ca content in leaves decreased significantly in the order of CdN, CdSN, CdF and CdS compared to Cd group (P < 0.05), with decremented rates of 15.2 %, 18.8 %, 20.1 % and 24.3 %, respectively. However, the Ca contents in roots increased significantly after FNMs treatment, with increased rate of 48.3 % by FCNs, 42.5 % by S,N- FCNs, 38.3 % by N-FCNs and 36.7 % by S-FCNs (P < 0.05). Significant differences were also shown in the contents of Fe in leaves and roots from Table 1 (P < 0.05). Although the leaf Fe content in CdF was significantly reduced by 19.3 %, which were significantly elevated in CdS (128 %), CdN (77.6 %) and CdSN (14.2 %) groups (P < 0.05). All the FNMs significantly reduced the content of Fe in roots, with decreased rate of 29.5 % to 54.2 % (P < 0.05). The Zn content of leaves or roots in CdF showed no significant differences compared to Cd. Although the Zn content in leaves was declined by 7.67 %, which in roots was raised by 10.7 % in CdS group. N-FCNs increased the Zn contents both in leaves and roots, with incremented rates of 15.3 % and 51.0 %, respectively. S,N-FCNs did not significantly influence the Zn content in leaves, but obviously decremented it in roots by 10.37 % (P < 0.05).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influences of FNMs on plant physiological processes
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 shows the influences of FNMs on the enzymatic and nonenzymatic antioxidant systems in Tagetes patula L. under different treatments. Cd toxicity disturbed the oxidant-antioxidant homeostasis in plant cells. Specifically, activities of SOD and CAT in roots were suppressed by Cd, but activities of POD and APX were raised significantly (P < 0.1). The AsA content in each treatment showed no significant difference. In leaves, Cd stress induced the increased activities of POD and APX and decreased content of AsA, but showed no significant influences on SOD and CAT activities (P < 0.1). Application of FNMs further influenced the antioxidant systems in plant. Compared with Cd group, the FNMs reduced SOD activities by 2.74 %−45.0 % in roots, particularly N-FCNs and S,N-FCNs. However, the SOD activity in leaves were enhanced with S-FCNs and N-FCNs by 11.9 % and 2.96 %, respectively. Although S-FCNs, N-FCNs and S,N-FCNs influence inapparently on CAT activity in roots, FCNs significantly increased which by 4.57 folds (P < 0.1). POD activities and APX activities in roots were reduced after FNMs treatment (Figs. 4c and 4d). However, POD activity in leaves was slightly raised by FCNs (7.00 %), and APX activities in leaves were significantly increased by 171 % and 84.3 % with S-FCNs and S,N-FCNs treatment (P < 0.1). Only N-FCNs notably increased AsA content by 52.8 % in roots, while S-FCNs, N-FCNs and S,N-FCNs increased which in leaves by 68.1 %, 98.9 % and 199 % (P＜0.1). Tagetes patula L. improved the total antioxidant capacity to cope with Cd toxicity in Cd group (Fig. 4f). FNMs additive influenced insignificantly on the total antioxidant capacity in root, however, FCNs significantly enhanced which in leaves by 29.0 % (Fig. 4f, P < 0.1).

The MDA content was used to assess membrane lipid peroxidation in Fig. 4g. Compared with CT, Cd stress increased the MDA contents in roots and leaves in Cd group. Although N-FCNs and S,N-FCNs increased the MDA contents in roots, FCNs and S-FCNs effectively decreased the MDA content of roots by 25.0 % and 14.6 %. Besides, all FNMs except S-FCNs lowered the level of MDA in leaves, with decreased rate of 27.9 % by FCNs, 15.5 % by FCNs and 23.1 % by S,N-FCNs.

Compared with the CT group, the contents of chlorophyll a (Chl-a), chlorophyll a (Chl-b) and total Chl in leaves were extremely reduced by 40.7, 33.4 % and 29.3 % under Cd stress ( Fig. 5a). Fortunately, FNMs mitigated the toxic effects of Cd on Chl. FCNs and S-FCNs significantly increased the content of Chl-a by 79.6 % and 67.1 %, although they had no significant effects on Chl-b content (P ＜ 0.1). N-FCNs increased Chl-a and Chl-b content by 28.3 % and 30.8 %, respectively. The contents of Chl-a and Chl-b in CdSN were maximumly incremented, with increased rates of 96.6 % and 95.9 %, respectively. Contents of the organic acids in roots, including oxalic acid (OA), malic acid (MA), lactic acid (LA), acetic acid (AA), maleic acid (MAA), citric acid (CA), fumaric acid (FM), and succinic acid (SA) are shown in Fig. 5b and S4. While LA, AA and SA were not detected in the roots treated by FCNs and S-FCNs, FCNs significantly increased the contents of OA, MA, MAA, CA and FM by 7.85 %, 70.3 %, 1.43 folds, 3.71 % and 58.9 %, respectively, comparing with the Cd group (P ＜ 0.05). S-FCNs significantly increased the contents of MA, MAA and FM by 79.8 %, 4.66 folds and 28.4 %, respectively, although decreased the content of OA by 24.7 % (P ＜ 0.05).

The genetic expression of HMA in Tagetes patula L. are shown in Fig. S5. In the phytoremediation process, the regulation effects of materials on HMA expression are opposite in roots and stems. FCNs dramatically reduced the expression of HMA in roots by 91.1 % although increased that in stems by 20.7 %. While S-FCNs incremented HMA expression in roots by 25.4 %, they extremely declined that in stems by 67.1 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influences of FNMs on soil properties of rhizosphere
ce:section.ce:para: The soil physicochemical property of rhizosphere in each treatment is shown in Table 2. Compared with CT, although the contents of AN, AP and AK in rhizosphere soils were higher than which in Cd group, the EC, CEC and SOC content were decreased. The soil pH was increased, however, FCNs and S-FCNs significantly decreased the soil pH by 1.04 % and 1.17 %, while N-FCNs increased soil pH by 4.82 %. Although FNMs had no significant effect on soil CEC, they significantly influenced EC of the Cd-polluted soils. Specifically, FCNs, S-FCNs and S,N-FCNs significantly incremented EC by 19.3 %, 102 % and 55.9 %, while N-FCNs reduced it by 19.0 % (P < 0.05). The fertility of Cd-polluted soils was also changed with FNMs application. Only N-FCNs significantly incremented SOC content by 10.0 % (P < 0.05). The AP contents were increased significantly by FCNs and S, N-FCNs with 10.4 % and 7.78 %, respectively (P < 0.05). Contents of AN and AK in rhizosphere soils were significantly raised by N-FCNs with 1.55 % and 15.7 % (P < 0.05), while which were decremented by FCNs, S-FCNs and S,N-FCNs.

As shown in Fig. 6, Cd stress significantly reduced the activity of S-CAT by 6.36 % when comparing Cd group with CT (P < 0.05). However, S-PPO activity changed little in Cd-polluted soils. FCNs significantly raised S-CAT activity by 6.26 % compared to Cd group (P < 0.05). On the contrary, S-CAT activities were insignificantly decreased in groups of CdS, CdN, and CdSN (P < 0.05). Despite S-FCNs and S,N-FCNs reduced S-PPO activity by 11.7 % and 15.1 %, N-FCNs significantly increased it by 12.6 % (P < 0.1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of FNMs on soil bacterial community structure in rhizosphere
ce:section.ce:para: Table 3 and Fig. 7, S7–10 show the structure and diversity of rhizosphere soil bacteria under different conditions. A total of 13,585 OUTs of bacterial community were detected by high-throughput sequencing, and 5.32 % OUT were shared by five groups (Fig. S7). The number of sequences treated with S-FCNs was the highest in the five treatments (Table 3). Chao1, Shannon, and Simpson indices were calculated to evaluate the alpha diversity of rhizosphere soil bacteria in CT, Cd, CdS, CdN, and CdSN groups. The coverage of Good in each soil sample was approximately 0.99, suggesting that the library truly reflects the sequencing depth of the soil bacterial community (Table 3). There were no significant differences in the Chao1 indices of FNMs treatments. The Shannon and Simpson indices of the soils polluted with Cd were significantly lower than those of the CT group (P ＜ 0.05, Table 3). FCNs and S-FCNs significantly lowered the Shannon indices compared to Cd group, while N-FCNs and S,N-FCNs changed the indices little. Besides, the Simpson indices of N-FCNs and S,N-FCNs treatments were significantly higher than that in Cd group, whereas which were almost unchanged in CdF and CdS (P ＜ 0.05).

The phylogenetic classification of sequence tags in phyla, classes, orders, families and genera of bacterial community are listed in Table 3. The result revealed that Cd pollution decreased the abundances of bacteria in rhizosphere soil. According to the results of OTU species annotation, twenty dominant relative abundances of soil bacteria at the phylum level are shown in Fig. S8. Proteobacteria, Actinobacteriota, Chloroflexi, Gemmatimonadota, and Bacteroidota were the dominant phyla in all soil samples, covering about 80 % of the taxonomic sequences. In Cd-polluted soils, the addition of FCNs, S-FCNs, N-FCNs and S,N-FCNs increased the relative abundance of Proteobacteria. FCNs decreased the relative abundance of Chloroflexi and Gemmatimonadota, and S-FCNs declined the relative abundance of Chloroflexi. Compared with CT, Cd pollution mainly increased the relative abundance of Bacteroidota. However, FNMs decreased the relative abundance of Bacteroidota. Gammaproteobacteria, Actinobacteria, Alphaproteobacteria, Bacteroidia and Gemmatimonadetes were the main taxa at the class level (Fig. S9). Cd increased the relative abundance of Gammaproteobacteria, Actinobacteria, Bacteroidia and Gemmatimonadetes in rhizosphere soils. FNMs incremented the relative abundance of Gammaproteobacteria but decremented the relative abundance of Bacteroidia. The relative abundances of Actinobacteria were incremented by the addition of FCNs. Moreover, N-FCNs increased the relative abundance of Gemmatimonadetes. In Fig. 7, the genera Aeromicrobium, Vicinamibacteraceae and Sphingomonas were inhibited by Cd toxicity. However, the Pseudonocardia abundance increased significantly under Cd stress. FCNs significantly increased the abundance of Luteimonas, Nocardioides, Aeromicrobium and Vicinamibacteraceae. S-FCNs significantly increased the population numbers of Luteimonas, Nocardioides and Pseudonocardia. N-FCNs significantly increased the population numbers of Aeromicrobium, Vicinamibacteraceae and KD4–96, but decreased the population of Pseudonocardia compared to Cd group. S,N-FCNs raised the abundance of genera Luteimonas, Nocardioides, Aeromicrobium and Vicinamibacteraceae. Fig. S10 shows the heat maps of several dominant genera, revealing the differences on soil bacterial genera among treatments.

The dynamics of microbial structure are presented in Fig. S11 and S12. The microbial diversity changed with increased remediation time. In Cd and CdS groups, the Chao1 index on the 47th day significantly increased compared with that on the 10th day. Similarly, the Shannon index and Simpson index in Cd and CdF groups also increased with times. Fig. S12 shows the changes of the top 20 microbial genera following the soil Cd concentration and time. With decreased Cd concentration in rhizosphere soils after remediation, the relative abundance of the top 20 genera increased, except in the Cd group. Although some specific genera are sensitive to Cd, their changes in abundance can be moderated by remediation of FNMs and Tagetes Patula L. For example, Cd suppressed the growth of Aeromicrobium, and Gitt-GS-136, especially at the beginning of phytoremediation. However, after 47 days remediation, the relative abundance of Aeromicrobium and Gitt-GS-136 increased. Although Streptomyces was not the predominant bacterial genus in the rhizosphere area of Tagetes Patula L. after 47 days remediation, FCNs mitigated the change in its abundance that triggered by Cd toxicity at the beginning of remediation. The relative abundance of some Cd-tolerance genera, such as Nocardioides and Luteimonas, were even promoted by FCNs and S-FCNs after remediation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussion
Material structure determine the regulation effects of FNMs on phytoremediation
ce:section.ce:para: Cd is a highly toxic heavy metals to organism even at low concentration, and its toxicity is easily amplified through the food chain [41]. Phytoremediation is an operationally simple, feasible, economically viable, aesthetically pleasing and publically accepted technology [42]. In the present study, Tagetes patula L. showed strong tolerance and excellent bioaccumulation ability to Cd with BCF (9.20) and TF (2.51) values greater than 1.00 (Fig. 3d). Therefore, Tagetes patula L. is a potential hyperaccumulator in phytoremediation of Cd-contaminated soil [43]. The result was consistent with the study in [44].

Considering the limitations of hyperaccumulator including slow growth, less biomass and time consuming process [42], FNMs were applied to enhance the phytoremediation efficiency of Tagetes patula L. After remediation by FNMs coupling with Tagetes patula L., Cd contents in soils decreased and the Cd removal efficiency improved, as shown in Fig. 3a-b. In particular, FCNs, S-FCNs and S,N-FCNs significantly enhanced the Cd removal efficiency by 15.9 %, 20.1 %, and 12.0 %, respectively. The removal efficiency of Cd is especially improved with FCNs and S-FCNs supplements. About 50 % of Cd in contaminated soils are remediated in CdF and CdS groups. However, the phytoremediation efficiency of Cd by Tagetes patula L. alone within Cd group is only 33.1 % in this study, and the highest removal efficiency of bioavailable Cd is 32.3 %, as reported by Yuan et al. [45]. FNMs also elevate the BCFs of Cd in the soil-plant system, which are much higher than the reported BCFs of Tagetes patula L [45,46]. In particular, S-FCNs addition significantly increased the BCF by 49.7 %. However, FNMs decreased the TFs of Cd in Tagetes patula L., with the opposite change trend to BCFs (Fig. 3d). Comprehensively considering biomass, remediation efficiency and BCF, S-FCNs had the best promotion effects on coupling Tagetes patula L. to remediate Cd-polluted soils.

The direct mechanism of FNMs influencing phytoremediation included their actions in activating Cd and carrying Cd in the soil-plant system, just like other NM (M [47,48]). On one hand, FNMs were rich in active groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, carbonyl, etc. (Fig. 1), which provided abundant reactive sites for heavy metals. Therefore, FNMs not only can regulate the adsorption-desorption processes to control the transformation and movement of Cd in soils, but also can immobilize Cd in vivo to reduce the toxicity of Cd to plants [24]. On the other hand, due to the adsorption characteristics derived from the effects of nano-size and functional groups, FNMs can act as nanocarriers to deliver cargoes to plants, thereby promoting the passive uptake of Cd by plants [24]. As a result, the different regulation effects of FNMs on phytoremediation can be attributed to their various components and surface structures.

Oxygen, nitrogen and sulfur containing functional groups are predominant for the adsorption to heavy metals, other functional groups (e.g., -CC, -CH2, and -O-CH3) can also act on heavy metals adsorption [49]. Our prior study demonstrated that the carboxyl-rich surface modification of FCNs was primarily responsible for their active redox characteristics, which further controlled plant responses and adaptions to biotic and abiotic stresses [28]. Besides, FCNs rich in carboxyl and hydroxy groups superiorly increased the bioavailability of Cd2+ and Pb2+ in aquatic environment compared to N-FCNs modified with -NH2 and NH4 + ([24]a). Therefore, FCNs improved the phytoremediation efficiency because of the increased biomass and Cd bioavailability. Sulfur containing functional groups were also reported to be beneficial to plant growth and materials’ adsorption of heavy metals [49]. In the present result, S-FCNs significantly promote the biomass accumulation of Tagetes patula L., thus improving the phytoextraction efficiency to Cd. However, the TF in Tagetes patula L. treated by S-FCNs declined significantly due to their immobilization effects on Cd in plant tissues. Fig. 3e also shown that S-FCNs induced the redistribution of Cd in cells, with more Cd stored in cell wall and organelle. The nitrogen containing functional groups, such as -NH2 and -NH-, contributed mostly to selective adsorption of heavy metals onto NMs, which were commonly synergistic with the oxygen containing functional groups [49]. The relative higher contents of carboxyl and amino groups resulted in the higher electronegativity and pH value of N-FCNs (Fig. 1). So, N-FCNs had strong absorption effects on Cd and their addition led to the fixation of Cd and other cations in the soils and root surface, which hindered the transport of both Cd and nutrient elements in the soil-plant system [24]. This further inhibited the plant growth and reduced the biomass of Tagetes patula L. (Fig. 2). The high plant Cd content in CdN was also due to the concentration effect caused by the declined biomass, which in turn aggravated the Cd toxicity to plant. S,N-FCNs had multiple sulfur and nitrogen containing groups. However, the carboxyl groups were relatively less (Fig. 1). So, although S,N-FCNs promoted the plant growth and Cd redistribution in cell walls, their effect on Cd remediation was lower than FCNs and S-FCNs (Fig. 2 & 3). These results demonstrated that the sulfur doping could effectively enhance the promotion effects of S-FCNs on phytoremediation, while the presence of large numbers of amino groups would aggravate the toxicity of Cd to plants.

As the potential risk is of great concern in the application of nanomaterials, the fate of FCNs-based materials in the soil-plant system was explored by Raman spectra, as shown in Fig. S6. Before cultivation, the G peaks around 1600 cm-1 (which are derived from the graphite structure) can be detected in spectra of soils supplemented with FCNs-based materials, whereas the G peaks vanished after 47-day cultivation. The results are in line with our previous studies [29], which demonstrated that FCNs-based materials might be transformed in soils and plants, resulting in no nanostructured substances remaining in soils and plants. The acute toxicity test of zebrafish also proved the biosafety of FCNs because their toxicity grades can be defined as “practically nontoxic”, which was reported in Chen et al. [50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The intrinsic mechanisms of FNMs regulating phytoremediation
MFCNs regulate the active absorption of Cd in plants
ce:section.ce:para: The unique surface structure of FNMs not only regulated the passive uptake and transport of Cd by plants, but also affected their active absorption to Cd from rhizosphere soils. There are numerous channel proteins, transporters and genes regulating the delivery of Cd in plants [51]. FNMs regulated Tagetes patula L.’ ability to actively absorb Cd via various physiological processes, such as root development, exudates and genetic expression. First, root biomass and volume influenced the root absorption function [52]. For example, S-FCNs and S,N-FCNs promoted root development and increased root volume. The contact area between the roots and the soil Cd increased, which further facilitated the removal of Cd from the soil (Fig. 2b).

Second, plants have been observed to release low molecular-weight organic acids (LMWOAs) into the rhizosphere to enhance the bioavailability and migration of heavy metals in the soil-plant system [53]. The desorption of heavy metals by different organic acids is different, which is due to the differences in the complexation strength between organic acids and heavy metals [54]. In addition, some LMWOAs, such as MA, can reduce Cd phytotoxicity by promoting plant growth, photosynthetic pigment synthesis, and root activity [55]. Compared to Cd group, FCNs increased the LMWOAs content in roots, especially the contents of MA, MAA, CA and FM. S-FCNs increased the contents of MA, MAA and FM, but didn’t increase the total LMWOAs content (Fig. 5b). The increased LMWOAs and root respiration contributed to the enhanced acidity of soils, thus improved the availability of Cd in soils [47].

Third, FNMs could regulate the genetic expression of heavy metals transporters, which affected the transport and distribution of Cd in plants [56]. HMA is a kind of protein that utilizes ATP hydrolysis to release energy to transport heavy metals cations across membranes [57]. For example, HMA2 and HMA3 that located at plasma membrane and tonoplast can act as heavy metals transporters to accelerate Cd migration from soils to roots and Cd transportation from stems to leaves [58]. FCNs inhibited the HMA expression in roots but promoted it in stems, while S-FCNs promoted it in roots but inhibited it in stems (Fig. S5). So, more Cd were fixed in roots by S-FCNs and then, however, FCNs promoted the upward transport of Cd from roots to leaves. The results may explain the changes in BCFs and TFs in CdF and CdS groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FNMs regulate plant to detoxify Cd toxicity
FNMs regulate plant growth to dilute Cd toxicity by regulating nutrient uptake and photosynthesis
ce:section.ce:para: Cd stress disrupts ionic balance and enzymatic processes, which ultimately result in a decrease of photosynthetic rate and biomass of plants [59]. NMs application may influence the antagonistic or cooperative uptake of bivalent nutrient element and heavy metals [60]. In FNMs treatments, the variation tendency of the contents of Fe and Ca in roots were converse to Cd. However, the change trend of Zn content was consistent with that of Cd (Table 1). The results indicated that FNMs could regulate the cooperative absorption to Cd, Fe and Ca, as well as the competitive absorption to Cd and Zn by roots. The contents of these divalent metals changed in the similar trends under FNMs application, except that S-FCNs relatively increased more Cd in plants (Table 1). The reason is speculated as that large numbers of Cd occupied the heavy metals transporters in plant, resulting in the lower transport efficiency for other divalent metals [61]. Plants have developed metal homeostasis systems to prevent excessive accumulation of free metal ions, such as Fe, Zn, Mn, and Cu [62]. As a vital microelement for plant, Fe contents in leaves were elevated by MFCNs, especially by S-FCNs with 128 %. The result was consistent with our earlier research, which demonstrated that FNMs can “smartly” amend the dysregulated Fe uptake in plants under stress [24].

Photosynthetic capacity can be reflected by the concentration of Chl, which is the main photosynthetic pigment and acts as a light‐harvesting antenna [63]. Cd significantly inhibited the synthesis of Chl, but FNMs increased the contents of Chl-a, Chl-b and total Chl in Tagetes patula L. and mitigated the adverse effects of Cd on photosynthesis (Fig. 5a). In particularly, FCNs, S-FCNs and S,N-FCNs dramatically improved the total Chl contents by 46.1 %, 34.6 % and 70.9 % under Cd stress (P < 0.1), and eventually contributing to the higher biomasses of plant (Figs. 2b and 5a). On one hand, the results were produced by the enhanced antioxidant system activity and the dilution of plant Cd contents with the increased biomass, which are triggered by the application of MFCNs [64]. On the other hand, CDs have also been reported to have light capturing ability, which can compound with chloroplasts to form a light conversion system to promote UV light absorption and electron transfer in the photosystem [63]. Besides, CDs can also induce the overexpression of genes related to Chl synthesis or increase ribulose‐1,5‐bisphosphate carboxylase/oxygenase (Rubisco) activity [65]. Thus, the photosynthetic activity and efficiency were improved, and the carbon reaction proceeding and biomass accumulate were accelerated ultimately [63]. The subcellular distribution of Cd in leaves was determined to further explore the influence of MFCNs on Cd biotoxicity and accumulation at the cellular level. As shown in Fig. 3, Cd was distributed mainly in the cell wall and organelle. S-FCNs and N-FCNs significantly increased the Cd content in organelle. This shift might due to the increased binding of Cd to the chloroplast in the leaves [17]. However, S-FCNs showed more positive effects on alleviating the Cd toxicity to chloroplast than N-FCNs, reflected by the higher Chl content in CdS group (Fig. 5a). S,N-FCNs inhibited the transportation of Cd into cells, as more Cd were immobilized on cell wall. Hence, the chloroplasts in CdSN were less poisoned by Cd and the Chl levels were the highest among the treatments. In general, FNMs, especially FCNs, S-FCNs and S,N-FCNs, can improve Chl content in plants to cope with Cd stress, and the subsequently increased biomass contributes to the dilution of Cd toxicity to plant, which in turn encouraged plant growth. Furthermore, the improved biomass also enhanced the phytoextraction efficiency in Tagetes patula L.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FCNs regulate plant internal redox homeostasis
ce:section.ce:para: Cd stress usually induces excess accumulation of ROS and damages internal redox homeostasis in plants, leading to oxidative damage to cell component and structure [66]. Then the nutrient uptake and metabolism processes are disrupted in plants [67,68]. To scavenge the overproduced ROS under Cd stress, plants have evolved a complex antioxidant defense system including enzymatic (such as SOD, CAT, POD, APX) and nonenzymatic (such as AsA) antioxidant defense systems [59]. FNMs application can influence plant antioxidant system responsible for detoxification. As an indicator of lipid peroxidation in plants, the accumulation of MDA is primarily caused by ROS, which reflects the level of oxidative stress in plants [69]. The MDA contents of Tagetes patula L. in Cd-contaminated soil were significantly higher than CT in both leaves and roots ( P < 0.1, Fig. 4g), indicating that plants had suffered severe oxidative damage [59]. Fortunately, FNMs moderated the oxidative damage on membrane in some degree [47]. Additions of FCNs and S-FCNs significantly decreased the MDA content in roots, reflecting their detoxification function to Cd toxicity (Fig. 4g). However, N-FCNs and S,N-FCNs aggravated membrane lipid peroxidation in roots, which may be related to the high residual Cd concentration and the destructed oxidation homeostasis [66]. Except for S-FCNs, FNMs including FCNs, N-FCNs and S,N-FCNs significantly relieved the membrane lipid peroxidation caused by Cd in leaves (P < 0.1). Particularly, FCNs reduced the most MDA level in leaves, because of the lowest Cd content (Fig. 3c) and the strongest antioxidant capacity (Fig. 4f). Although the Cd content in CdS group was extremely higher than that in Cd group, the MDA level showed no significant difference in the leaf. This also proved the positive effects of S-FCNs on plant antioxidant system, especially on APX activity in Fig. 4d.

The regulation effects of FNMs on plant antioxidant system can also influence the internal redox homeostasis of Tagetes patula L. exposed to Cd toxicity (Fig. 4). SOD is generally considered the first line of defense against superoxide to catalyze the dismutation of harmful superoxide radical (O2•−) to H2O2 and water. Subsequently, detoxification pathways involved CAT, POD and AsA/GSH cycle are activated to transform H2O2 to water [69]. Specifically, the first pathway involves CAT and POD, which converts H2O2 into water. The second pathway is the ascorbate-glutathione cycle, where H2O2 is scavenged by APX [69]. Because of the high concentration of Cd in roots, the activities of SOD, CAT were suppressed severely by Cd toxicity, and especially CAT activity was reduced by 84.5 % compared to CT (Fig. 4b). To maintain the antioxidant homeostasis, the POD and APX activities in roots were activated to scavenge ROS in Cd group. Among the four FNMs, only FCNs significantly improved CAT activity compared to Cd, with the increased rate of 4.57-fold (Fig. 4b). This may be attributed to the lower Cd content in roots in CdF compared to other treatments (Fig. 3c). In leaves, S-FCNs and S,N-FCNs obviously improved APX activity by 171 % and 84.3 %, which greatly promoted the AsA/GSH cycle in leaves [29]. Moreover, all the FNMs raised AsA contents in Tagetes patula L. exposed to Cd. Due to the complex interactions between MFCNs and antioxidant systems, the total antioxidant capacity was used to evaluated the overall influences of FNMs on plant antioxidant system. The result in Fig. 4f showed that the total antioxidant capacity was notably elevated only by FCNs (P < 0.1). While S-FCNs and S,N-FCNs did not obviously influence the total antioxidant capacity, N-FCNs inhibited which significantly because of the negative effect by the high level of Cd in CdN. As a result, the promotion effect of FCNs on plant antioxidant system was stronger than other FNMs, which was one of the key factors to improve the phytoremediation efficiency of Tagetes patula L. in Cd contaminated soils.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The extrinsic mechanisms of FCNs regulating phytoremediation
MFCNs regulate soil physicochemical properties and nutrient cycling in rhizosphere
ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metals pollution not only inhibit plant growth but also influence the soil environment, including physicochemical properties, enzyme activities, and microbial communities [5]. Soil physicochemical properties largely determine Cd bioavailability [70]. After entering into soil, the main fraction of Cd is complexed with soil particles, organic matter, biomass and other substances in soil, leading to different Cd forms including ion exchange state, carbonate binding state, humic acid binding state, iron and manganese oxide binding state, organic binding state and residue state [66]. Among them, Cd in exchange state shows the highest mobility and bioavailability [71]. In the soil environment, real-time transformation occurs among different forms of Cd and in dynamic equilibrium [71]. This equilibrium is affected by various soil factors, including soil pH, redox potential, CEC, organic matters, mineral elements, biological activities and their secretions (Hussain et al., 2021). The bioavailability of Cd in soils is negatively correlated with soil pH and CEC [72,73]. The soil organism including plants and microorganisms, can reduce the pH of the growth media by producing organic acids and CO2, thus influencing the occurrences of Cd in soils [74]. For instance, plants secrete low-molecular-weight organic acids, leading to the formation of organic acid/amino acid - metal/mineral complexes, or acidification induced by H+ release [70]. Compared with Cd, the soil pH decreased in CdF and CdS (Table 2). The decreased pH facilitated the bioavailability and mobilization of Cd in soils, thereby increasing the phytoextraction of Cd by Tagetes patula L. This was because that the negatively charged functional groups (e.g., phenolic, hydroxyl and carboxyl) in soils and root exudates were weakened to bind soil H+ under low pH conditions [70].

The available macro elements, including AN, AP, and AK, are essential to plant physiological process. Consequently, plants absorb and utilize significant quantities of these elements during their growth and development [75]. However, Cd toxicity inhibited the growth and flowering of Tagetes patula L., leading to reduced nutrient consumption and thereby resulting in higher levels of AN, AP, and AK in rhizosphere soils (Table 2). Besides, FCNs, S-FCNs, and S,N-FCNs reduced the contents of AN and AK in the rhizosphere soils, perhaps also due to the greater nutrient consumption resulting from the increased plant biomass [76]. Soil enzymes can facilitate electron transfer (e.g., redox reactions) and stimulate microbial activities (including growth, reproduction and function), which are responsible to nutrient cycle, plant tolerance and phytoremediation [70]. Thus, soil enzyme activity is not only one of the indicators of soil quality and soil fertility, but also plays a role in promoting benign ecological cycles in soil and preventing pests and diseases [29]. Soil enzyme activity is vital for promoting nutrient cycling and plant growth [77]. FNMs amendment mitigated the inhibitory effect of Cd on the enzymatic activities of ALP and UE in rhizosphere soils (Fig. S13). Furthermore, soil organic matter is known to chelate heavy metals and decrease the availability of metals to plant roots [77]. This synergistic relation among the S-CAT and S-PPO also leads to a speedy remediation of the heavy metals content by altering the oxidation state of the heavy metals in the soil [78]. By comparison, S-CAT was more sensitive to Cd and FNMs with the significantly decreased S-CAT activities in Cd, CdS, CdN and CdSN groups. However, FCNs increased the activity of S-CAT by 6.26 %. S-PPO activities were suppressed by S-FCNs and SN-FCNs, although N-FCNs increased it. N-FCNs attributed to the highest SOC content and inhibited Cd absorption by roots. FCNs increased the activities of S-CAT and S-PPO, then their synergistic effect promoted Cd remediation in soils. In general, FCNs were more friendly to soil enzymes than other FNMs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MFCNs regulate the microecological environment around rhizosphere
ce:section.ce:para: The rhizosphere is an area with a strong chemical relationship between plant roots and soil microorganisms [79]. The interaction between plant roots and soil microorganisms results in a synergistic relationship that can increase the yield and productivity of plants. This relationship can also help plants withstand various stresses [80]. Microorganism is an important part of soil and plays a vital role in stabilizing and regulating soil ecological functions, such as soil fertility, nutrient uptake by plant roots, and transformation of heavy metals [66,81]. The microorganism-mediated transformation of Cd in soils could be conducted through redox reaction, methylation, biosorption, bio-accumulation, excretion of chelating compounds, and interaction with plant roots [82]. In the rhizosphere of plants, thriving microbial communities with substantial specific surface areas and dynamic metabolic activity can influence the chemical speciation and bioavailability of Cd through various pathways, thereby affecting Cd accumulation in plants [83]. Heavy metals pollution in soils usually stimulates metal-tolerant species and inhibits metal-sensitive species, thus affecting the soil microbial community structure [84]. Therefore, the microbial diversity and structure evolved following with the Cd concentration in soils (Fig. S11-12). FNMs assisted phytoremediation decreased the Cd concentration, thereby improving the microbial diversity and abundance in the rhizosphere of Tagetes Patula L. The relative abundance of some Cd-tolerance genera, such as Nocardioides and Luteimonas, were even promoted by FCNs and S-FCNs after remediation.

For the Cd-polluted soils after remediation, there was no significant difference in the number of OUT among different treatments (P < 0.05, Table 3). Coverage index was used to represent the coverage of sample sequencing. The sequencing results were good because all the coverage indexes reached more than 0.99 (Table 3). The Shannon index represents the richness and evenness of the microbial community, while the Chao 1 and Simpson indices provide information on species richness and diversity [69]. By comparison, Simpson diversity index is more focused on relative abundance, while Shannon index is more focused on species richness [30]. In this study, there was no significant difference in the chao1 index among different treatments according to Table 3 (P < 0.05), indicating that Cd or MFCNs addition influenced indistinctively on the richness of soil bacterial communities. However, compared with the CT group, the Shannon index and Simpson index of Cd group significantly decreased (P < 0.05), indicating that Cd reduced the diversity and evenness of microorganisms in the Cd-contaminated soils. N-FCNs and S,N-FCNs increased the Simpson indices compared to Cd group, indicating that these two materials improved the bacterial diversities in rhizosphere soil. Besides, long-term remediation increased the bacterial community richness compared to short-term remediation (Fig. S11).

The diversity and abundance of the sensitive microbial species in soils will decrease under heavy metals pollution for a certain period of time. However, the abundance of microbe that are tolerant to heavy metals will increase due to their good adaption to heavy metals. All these factors will lead to changes in microbial community structure [85]. S-FCNs increased the abundance of Proteobacteria, FCNs application increased the total relative abundance of phyla Bacteroidota (Fig. S8), which belonged to the plant growth promoting rhinoacteria (PGPRs) and was correlated with phosphorus dissolution and polysaccharide degradation [86] . At genus level, although Cd toxicity inhibited the bacteria growth with the decreased relative abundance of the top 20 genus in Cd group (Fig. 7), it induced the increased relative abundance of the Cd-tolerant bacteria in rhizosphere soils, such as Nocardioides, and Pseudonocardia that involved in the P cycling [87,88], Anaerolineaceae, JG30-KF-CM45, Nocardioidaceae, Devosia, Flavobacterium, Nocardioides that involved in the N cycling [70,89-93]. Theses PGPRs are beneficial for soil tolerance and nutrient circle, thereby can improve the availability of nutrients in rhizosphere soils. FNMs improved the relative abundances of the top 20 genera in Cd-polluted soils, especially some dominant genera including Aeromicrobium, Vicinamibacteraceae, and Gemmatimonas. Heavy metals-reducing bacteria including Aeromicrobium, Pseudonocardia, and Gemmatimonas were capable of removing heavy metals in heavy metals-contaminated soils [94,95]. They could work together to remove metal contaminants effectively from soil [95]. Cd-tolerant bacteria, such as Vicinamibacteraceae, KD4–9, and Luteimonas, were highly resistant and adaptable to toxic environment and performed crucial roles in the transport and metabolism of various substrates [95,96]. Nocardioides can secrete carbohydrate to resist Cd stress, and enhanced v2 vibration of CO3 2- in soil carbonate. In the metal-polluted soil, Nocardioides overexpresses the metal resistance genes and plays a role in growth-promoting to promote phytoextraction [97]. FCNs raised the relative abundances of Luteimonas, Nocardioides, Aeromicrobium, and Vicinamibacteraceae. S-FCNs increased the relative abundances of genera Luteimonas, Nocardioides, and Pseudonocardia, thus improving the self-purification capacity of soils to Cd. N-FCNs induced the most abundant genera Aeromicrobium. Pseudonocardia may be sensitive to N element, as its relative abundance was decreased by the N doped N-FCNs and S,N-FCNs. The interaction between plant roots and soil microorganisms resulted in a synergistic relationship that could increase the growth and productivity of plants [80]. PGPRs can promote plant growth and active defense systems in plants [80]. In this study, MFCNs altered the physicochemical properties of the rhizosphere soil by promoting different dominant bacteria in the rhizosphere. Furthermore, promoting the synergistic relationship between plant roots and soil microorganisms can improve plant biomass and soil Cd remediation efficiency. In generally, FNMs can recruit potential beneficial bacteria in Cd-polluted rhizosphere soils, especially promote the abundance of Cd-reducing bacteria and Cd-tolerant bacteria, thus promoting the growth and bioaccumulation of Tagetes patula L.

In general, FNMs regulate the phytoremediation process of Tagetes patula L. with both direct interface chemical reaction and indirect biophysiological effects. The intrinsic and extrinsic mechanisms by which FNMs enhance the phytoremediation efficiency and Cd tolerance of Tagetes patula L. are shown in Fig. 8.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: The study showed that FNMs could effectively cooperated with Tagetes patula L. to remove Cd in rhizosphere soils. The mechanisms of FNMs regulating phytoremediation involved the direct interfacial chemical reaction with Cd, as well as the indirect internal and external effects on plant. On one hand, the nano-size effect and functional groups contributed FNMs to regulate the bioavailability, uptake and transport of Cd in soil-plant system. On the other hand, FNMs influenced some biological and environmental factors, including the plant growth and tolerance, detoxification processes, bioaccumulation capacity, as well as the rhizosphere soil property and microbial structure. In addition, the regulation effects of FNMs on phytoremediation depended on their unique surface structures. The cooperative remediation efficiency of S-FCNs with Tagetes patula L. maximumly reached 53.2 % in the Cd-contaminated soils, mainly due to the multiple S-containing groups adjusting Cd transportation in the soil-plant system and promoting plant biomass. This study provided a scientific basis for the application of FNMs, especially S-FCNs coupling Tagetes patula L. to remediate Cd-contaminated soil.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Environmental implication
ce:section.ce:para: Soil heavy metal contamination is a major global environmental problem. Cd has attracted widest attention in soil science due to its extremely toxicity, un-degradability, and high-mobility in the soil-plant system. As a typical hazardous material, Cd remediation in contaminated soils is urgent. This study employed functional carbon nanodots as a “nanotool” to promote the phytoremediation of Cd-polluted soils, and revealed the synergistic promotion mechanism and structure-function relationship of nonmetallic atoms doped carbon nanodots driving Tagetes patula L. to remediate Cd-contaminated soils. The results provide new insights into the fate of nanomaterials and Cd in the soil-plant system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Statement of novelty
ce:section.ce:para: Soil heavy metal contamination is a major global environmental problem. Cd has attracted widest attention in soil science due to its extremely toxicity, un-degradability, and high-mobility in the soil-plant system. As a typical hazardous material, Cd remediation in contaminated soils is urgent.

This study employed functional carbon nanodots as a “nanotool” to promote the phytoremediation of Cd-polluted soils, and revealed the synergistic promotion mechanism and structure-function relationship of nonmetallic atoms doped carbon nanodots driving Tagetes patula L. to remediate Cd-contaminated soils. The results provide new insights into the fate of nanomaterials and Cd in the soil-plant system.
